era_context: The early 11th century continues with the Song Dynasty in China, the Fatimid Caliphate in the Islamic world, the Ghaznavid Empire at its height, and Western Europe in the early feudal period. This is a time of significant cultural and technological advancement, religious ferment, and military competition between major powers. The period is marked by the expansion of long-distance trade, the development of feudal institutions, and the growth of monastic reform movements.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no Old World written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe beyond Rus' and Scandinavia
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse documentation)

events.title: Council of Seligenstadt (or Synod of Aachen) on ecclesiastical reform
events.description: A major ecclesiastical council or synod was held in the Frankish territories (possibly at Seligenstadt or Aachen) to address issues of monastic and clerical reform. This council was part of the broader Cluniac reform movement, which sought to restore discipline and piety to the Church. The council addressed issues such as clerical celibacy, simony (the sale of church offices), and monastic discipline. These reform efforts were foundational to the later Gregorian Reform movement of the late 11th century.

sources.name: Thietmar of Merseburg, Chronicon, written early 11th century

events.certainty_note: Ecclesiastical councils and synods of this period are often poorly dated in sources. The specific council of 1027 is attested in some sources, but the exact location and date are uncertain (±2–3 years).
cross_references: Part of broader Cluniac reform movement (10th–11th centuries)

events.title: Coronation of Conrad II as Holy Roman Emperor in Rome
events.description: Conrad II (Conrad the Salian), who had been elected King of the Romans in 1024, was crowned Holy Roman Emperor in Rome in 1027. This coronation legitimized his rule and marked the beginning of the Salian dynasty's dominance in the Holy Roman Empire. Conrad II's reign (1024–1039) was marked by efforts to strengthen imperial authority, reform the Church, and manage conflicts with the papacy and the nobility. His coronation was a significant moment in the consolidation of the Holy Roman Empire as a major European power.

sources.name: Wipo, Gesta Chuonradi Imperatoris (Deeds of Emperor Conrad), written 11th century

events.certainty_note: Conrad II's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in 1027 is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources and is not disputed by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Part of the Salian dynasty's rise to power

events.title: Death of Anund Jacob of Sweden
events.description: Anund Jacob (also called Anund the Great), King of Sweden, died in 1027 or 1028. His reign (c. 1015–1027) was marked by efforts to Christianize Sweden and to consolidate royal power. Anund Jacob was known for his military campaigns against the pagan Sami peoples and for his alliance with other Scandinavian rulers. His death marked a transition in Swedish politics and was followed by a period of succession disputes.
key_figures: Anund Jacob (King of Sweden, r. c. 1015–1027)
Olof Skötkonung (father, King of Sweden, r. c. 995–1022)

sources.name: Adam of Bremen, Gesta Hammaburgensis Ecclesiae Pontificum (History of the Archbishops of Hamburg-Bremen), written late 11th century
Snorri Sturluson, Heimskringla (sagas of Norwegian kings), written 13th century

events.certainty_note: Anund Jacob's death is attested in later Scandinavian sources, but the precise year is uncertain. Sources vary between 1027 and 1028. The event itself is not disputed, but dating is approximate.
cross_references: Part of broader Christianization of Scandinavia
Contemporary with Conrad II's rise to power in the Holy Roman Empire

disconfirming_evidence: The specific council or synod of 1027 is poorly attested; sources are vague about its location and exact date. The council may have occurred in 1026 or 1028 instead. Anund Jacob's death is dated variously to 1027 or 1028 in different sources, reflecting the uncertainty of Scandinavian chronology in this period. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of these events, but precision should not be overstated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1027 is moderate but regionally variable. Western European sources (German and Italian chronicles) provide good coverage of Conrad II's coronation. Scandinavian history is documented primarily through later sagas and chronicles written 200+ years after events, introducing significant chronological uncertainty. Ecclesiastical sources document reform efforts, though specific councils are often poorly dated. Islamic, Byzantine, and East Asian sources are available but less detailed for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe remain poorly documented. The surviving record is heavily biased toward elite political and religious events.

graph_edges.note: Early 11th-century ecclesiastical reform efforts laid the groundwork for the Gregorian Reform
Conrad II's establishment of imperial authority set the stage for later conflicts between emperors and popes
Scandinavian political transitions occurred contemporaneously with Norman expansion in Western Europe
